docs.title: TwoDEngine Documentation
docs.text: Welcome to the TwoDEngine documentation! TwoDEngine is a powerful 2D game engine built with modern C++ and designed for creating high-performance 2D games.

docs.title: 🎮 What is TwoDEngine?
docs.text: TwoDEngine is a comprehensive 2D game development framework that combines the power of SDL2 for graphics and input, with modern tools like ImGui for debugging, Lua/Sol2 for scripting, and Python/pybind11 for extensibility. It provides a solid foundation for building 2D games with a focus on performance, flexibility, and ease of use.

docs.title: ✨ Key Features
docs.text:
• SDL2 Integration: Hardware-accelerated 2D rendering with SDL2
  • ImGui Support: Built-in debugging and development UI
  • Scripting Support:
  • Lua scripting with Sol2 for game logic
  • Python integration with pybind11 for tools and extensions
  • Math Library: GLM (OpenGL Mathematics) for vector and matrix operations
  • Asset Management: Support for images, fonts, sounds, and tilemaps
  • Cross-Platform: Works on Windows, Linux, and macOS
  • Modern C++: Built with C++17 standards

docs.text:

docs.title: Minimum Requirements
docs.text:
• OS: Windows 10/11, Linux (Ubuntu 20.04+), macOS 10.15+
  • Compiler: GCC 7+, Clang 6+, MSVC 2019+, MinGW-w64
  • RAM: 4 GB
  • Storage: 500 MB for engine and dependencies

docs.title: Recommended Requirements
docs.text:
• OS: Windows 11, Linux (Ubuntu 22.04+), macOS 12+
  • Compiler: GCC 11+, Clang 14+, MSVC 2022+
  • RAM: 8 GB or more
  • Storage: 1 GB for engine, dependencies, and development tools

docs.title: 🛠️ Technology Stack
docs.text:
TwoDEngine is built on top of industry-standard libraries:

Library Purpose Version SDL2 Graphics, Input, Audio 2.0+ SDL2_image Image Loading 2.0+ SDL2_ttf Font Rendering 2.0+ SDL2_mixer Audio Mixing 2.0+ ImGui Debug UI 1.89+ GLM Math Library 0.9.9+ Lua Scripting 5.4+ Sol2 Lua Binding 3.3+ Python Extensibility 3.10+ pybind11 Python Binding 2.11+

docs.title: 🎯 Next Steps

Tip: This documentation is searchable! Use the search bar at the top to quickly find what you're looking for.

docs.title: Game Class
docs.text: The Game class is the core of TwoDEngine, managing the entire game lifecycle from initialization to shutdown.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Game class implements the main game loop pattern and coordinates all engine subsystems. It provides a simple interface for creating games while handling complex initialization and management tasks behind the scenes.

docs.title: Class Declaration
docs.text:
/**
 * @file Game.h
 * @brief Core game engine class definition
 */

class Game
{
public:
    Game();
    ~Game();

    void Initialize();
    void Run();
    void ProcessInput();
    void Update();
    void Render();
    void Destroy();

private:
    // Private implementation details
};

docs.title: Game()
docs.text:
Game();

Description: Constructs a new Game instance.

Parameters: None

Example: 

Game game;  // Create game instance

Notes: - The constructor performs minimal initialization - Heavy initialization is deferred to Initialize() - Safe to create multiple Game instances (though typically only one is needed)

docs.title: ~Game()
docs.text:
~Game();

Description: Destroys the Game instance and releases resources.

Parameters: None

Example: 

{
    Game game;
    // Game is automatically destroyed when going out of scope
}

Notes: - Automatically calls cleanup code - Ensures proper resource deallocation - Safe to call even if Initialize() was never called

docs.title: Initialize()
docs.text:
void Initialize();

Description: Initializes all game engine subsystems.

This method sets up: - SDL2 video and audio systems - Window and rendering context - Asset management system - Input handling system - Scripting engines (Lua and Python)

Parameters: None

Returns: void

Throws: May throw exceptions if initialization fails

Example: 

Game game;
game.Initialize();

Best Practices: 

Game game;
try {
    game.Initialize();
    game.Run();
} catch (const std::exception& e) {
    std::cerr << "Initialization failed: " << e.what() << std::endl;
    return 1;
}
game.Destroy();

Notes: - Must be called before Run() - Should only be called once per Game instance - Calling multiple times without Destroy() may cause resource leaks

See Also: Destroy()

docs.title: Run()
docs.text:
void Run();

Description: Starts the main game loop.

The game loop continuously: 1. Processes input events 2. Updates game logic 3. Renders the current frame 4. Maintains target frame rate

Parameters: None

Returns: void

Example: 

Game game;
game.Initialize();
game.Run();  // Blocks until game exits
game.Destroy();

Game Loop Structure: 

while (isRunning)
{
    ProcessInput();  // Handle user input
    Update();        // Update game state
    Render();        // Draw to screen

    // Frame rate limiting
    CapFrameRate();
}

Notes: - This method blocks until the game exits - The loop runs at the target frame rate (typically 60 FPS) - Exit the loop by setting isRunning = false

See Also: ProcessInput(), Update(), Render()

docs.title: ProcessInput()
docs.text:
void ProcessInput();

Description: Processes all input events for the current frame.

Handles: - Keyboard input - Mouse input - Controller input - Window events (close, resize, etc.)

Parameters: None

Returns: void

Example: 

void Game::ProcessInput()
{
    SDL_Event event;
    while (SDL_PollEvent(&event))
    {
        switch (event.type)
        {
            case SDL_QUIT:
                isRunning = false;
                break;
            case SDL_KEYDOWN:
                HandleKeyPress(event.key.keysym.sym);
                break;
            // Handle other events...
        }
    }
}

Typical Usage: 

// In your game loop
while (isRunning)
{
    ProcessInput();  // Called first each frame
    Update();
    Render();
}

Notes: - Called once per frame at the start of the game loop - Processes all pending events in the SDL event queue - Should be fast to avoid input lag

See Also: Run()

docs.title: Update()
docs.text:
void Update();

Description: Updates game logic for the current frame.

This method handles: - Game state updates - Physics simulation - AI logic - Animation updates - Collision detection - Script execution

Parameters: None

Returns: void

Example: 

void Game::Update()
{
    // Update delta time
    float deltaTime = CalculateDeltaTime();

    // Update game entities
    for (auto& entity : entities)
    {
        entity.Update(deltaTime);
    }

    // Update physics
    physicsWorld.Step(deltaTime);

    // Check collisions
    collisionSystem.CheckCollisions();
}

Delta Time Pattern: 

void Game::Update()
{
    // Calculate time since last frame
    Uint32 currentTime = SDL_GetTicks();
    float deltaTime = (currentTime - lastFrameTime) / 1000.0f;
    lastFrameTime = currentTime;

    // Use deltaTime for frame-rate independent updates
    playerPosition += playerVelocity * deltaTime;
}

Notes: - Called once per frame after ProcessInput() - Should use delta time for frame-rate independent behavior - Keep updates efficient to maintain target frame rate

See Also: Run(), Render()

docs.title: Render()
docs.text:
void Render();

Description: Renders the current frame to the screen.

This method: 1. Clears the screen 2. Renders all game objects 3. Renders UI elements 4. Presents the frame to the display

Parameters: None

Returns: void

Example: 

void Game::Render()
{
    // Clear screen
    SDL_SetRenderDrawColor(renderer, 0, 0, 0, 255);
    SDL_RenderClear(renderer);

    // Render game objects
    for (auto& entity : entities)
    {
        entity.Render(renderer);
    }

    // Render UI
    RenderUI();

    // Present frame
    SDL_RenderPresent(renderer);
}

Rendering Order: 

void Game::Render()
{
    // 1. Clear screen
    ClearScreen();

    // 2. Render background
    RenderBackground();

    // 3. Render game world (sorted by depth)
    RenderGameWorld();

    // 4. Render particles/effects
    RenderEffects();

    // 5. Render UI (always on top)
    RenderUI();

    // 6. Present to screen
    PresentFrame();
}

Notes: - Called once per frame after Update() - Should only contain rendering code, no game logic - Render order matters for proper layering

See Also: Run(), Update()

docs.title: Destroy()
docs.text:
void Destroy();

Description: Cleans up and releases all engine resources.

This method: - Destroys all game entities - Releases loaded assets - Shuts down SDL subsystems - Closes scripting engines - Frees allocated memory

Parameters: None

Returns: void

Example: 

Game game;
game.Initialize();
game.Run();
game.Destroy();  // Clean up before exit

Best Practice with RAII: 

int main(int argc, char* argv[])
{
    try {
        Game game;
        game.Initialize();
        game.Run();
        game.Destroy();
    } catch (const std::exception& e) {
        std::cerr << "Error: " << e.what() << std::endl;
        return 1;
    }
    return 0;
}

Notes: - Should be called before program exit - Safe to call multiple times - Automatically called by destructor if not called explicitly

See Also: Initialize()

docs.title: Basic Game
docs.text:
#include "Game.h"
#include <iostream>

int main(int argc, char* argv[])
{
    std::cout << "Starting TwoDEngine..." << std::endl;

    // Create game instance
    Game game;

    // Initialize engine
    game.Initialize();

    // Run game loop
    game.Run();

    // Clean up
    game.Destroy();

    std::cout << "Game exited successfully." << std::endl;
    return 0;
}

docs.title: With Error Handling
docs.text:
#include "Game.h"
#include <iostream>
#include <exception>

int main(int argc, char* argv[])
{
    try {
        Game game;

        std::cout << "Initializing engine..." << std::endl;
        game.Initialize();

        std::cout << "Starting game..." << std::endl;
        game.Run();

        std::cout << "Shutting down..." << std::endl;
        game.Destroy();

        return 0;
    }
    catch (const std::exception& e) {
        std::cerr << "Fatal error: " << e.what() << std::endl;
        return 1;
    }
    catch (...) {
        std::cerr << "Unknown fatal error occurred" << std::endl;
        return 1;
    }
}

docs.title: Implementation Status
docs.text: Method Status Notes Game() ✅ Implemented Basic constructor ~Game() ✅ Implemented Basic destructor Initialize() 🚧 Stub Needs SDL initialization Run() 🚧 Stub Needs game loop implementation ProcessInput() 🚧 Stub Needs input handling Update() 🚧 Stub Needs game logic Render() 🚧 Stub Needs rendering code Destroy() 🚧 Stub Needs cleanup code

docs.title: Design Patterns
docs.text: The Game class implements several design patterns:

docs.title: Singleton Pattern (Optional)
docs.text:
class Game
{
public:
    static Game& GetInstance()
    {
        static Game instance;
        return instance;
    }

private:
    Game() = default;
    Game(const Game&) = delete;
    Game& operator=(const Game&) = delete;
};

docs.title: Game Loop Pattern
docs.text:
void Game::Run()
{
    while (isRunning)
    {
        ProcessInput();  // Input phase
        Update();        // Update phase
        Render();        // Render phase
    }
}

docs.title: Performance Considerations
docs.text:
• Frame Rate: Target 60 FPS (16.67ms per frame)
  • Update Budget: ~10ms for Update()
  • Render Budget: ~6ms for Render()
  • Input Budget: ~1ms for ProcessInput()

docs.title: Thread Safety
docs.text:
The Game class is not thread-safe. All methods should be called from the main thread only.

For multi-threaded operations: - Use a separate thread for asset loading - Use a separate thread for physics simulation - Synchronize with the main thread using mutexes

docs.title: Future Enhancements
docs.text:
• Add configuration file support
  • Implement scene management
  • Add profiling and debugging tools
  • Support for multiple windows
  • Hot-reloading of assets and scripts

Source Code

View the complete source code: src/Game.h | src/Game.cpp

docs.title: API Reference Overview
docs.text: Welcome to the TwoDEngine API Reference. This section provides detailed documentation for all classes, methods, and functions in the engine.

docs.title: Game Engine Core
docs.text: Class Description Status Game Main game engine class managing the game lifecycle ✅ Implemented Window Window management and rendering context 🚧 Planned Renderer 2D rendering system 🚧 Planned AssetManager Asset loading and management 🚧 Planned

docs.title: Entity Component System
docs.text: Class Description Status Entity Base entity class 🚧 Planned Component Base component class 🚧 Planned System Base system class 🚧 Planned Registry Entity-component registry 🚧 Planned

docs.title: Graphics
docs.text: Class Description Status Sprite 2D sprite rendering 🚧 Planned Texture Texture management 🚧 Planned Animation Sprite animation system 🚧 Planned Camera 2D camera system 🚧 Planned

docs.title: Input
docs.text: Class Description Status InputManager Input handling system 🚧 Planned Keyboard Keyboard input 🚧 Planned Mouse Mouse input 🚧 Planned Gamepad Controller input 🚧 Planned

docs.title: Audio
docs.text: Class Description Status AudioManager Audio system management 🚧 Planned Sound Sound effect playback 🚧 Planned Music Background music playback 🚧 Planned

docs.title: Scripting
docs.text: Class Description Status LuaScript Lua script execution 🚧 Planned PythonScript Python script execution 🚧 Planned ScriptManager Script management 🚧 Planned

docs.title: API Documentation Generation
docs.text: The API documentation is generated from source code comments using Doxygen. This ensures the documentation stays in sync with the code.

docs.title: Viewing Full API Documentation
docs.text:
To generate and view the complete API documentation:

# Generate Doxygen documentation
doxygen Doxyfile

# Open the documentation
# Windows
start docs/api/html/index.html

# Linux
xdg-open docs/api/html/index.html

# macOS
open docs/api/html/index.html

docs.title: Method Documentation
docs.text:
All public methods are documented with:

  • Brief description: One-line summary
  • Detailed description: Comprehensive explanation
  • Parameters: All parameters with types and descriptions
  • Return value: Return type and description
  • Exceptions: Any exceptions that may be thrown
  • Example: Usage example code
  • See also: Related methods or classes

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
/**
 * @brief Initialize the game engine
 *
 * This method initializes all engine subsystems including:
 * - SDL2 video and audio
 * - Window and renderer
 * - Asset manager
 * - Input system
 *
 * @return true if initialization succeeded, false otherwise
 *
 * @throws std::runtime_error if SDL initialization fails
 *
 * @note This method must be called before Run()
 * @warning Calling Initialize() multiple times without Destroy() may cause memory leaks
 *
 * @see Run(), Destroy()
 *
 * @code
 * Game game;
 * if (game.Initialize()) {
 *     game.Run();
 *     game.Destroy();
 * }
 * @endcode
 */
bool Initialize();

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
Each class documentation includes:

  1. Basic usage example: Simple, minimal code
  2. Advanced example: Real-world usage scenario
  3. Best practices: Recommended patterns
  4. Common pitfalls: What to avoid

docs.title: API Stability
docs.text:
TwoDEngine follows semantic versioning:

  • Major version (X.0.0): Breaking API changes
  • Minor version (0.X.0): New features, backward compatible
  • Patch version (0.0.X): Bug fixes, backward compatible

docs.title: API Stability Indicators
docs.text:
• ✅ Stable: API is stable and won't change
  • ⚠️ Experimental: API may change in future versions
  • 🚧 Planned: Feature is planned but not yet implemented
  • ❌ Deprecated: API will be removed in future versions

docs.title: Contributing to API Documentation
docs.text:
When contributing code to TwoDEngine, please:

  1. Document all public APIs: Use Doxygen comment style
  2. Include examples: Add usage examples in comments
  3. Update this overview: Add new classes to the tables above
  4. Generate docs: Run Doxygen to verify formatting
  5. Test examples: Ensure code examples compile and run

See the Contributing Guide for more details.

docs.title: API Design Principles
docs.text:
TwoDEngine APIs follow these principles:

  1. Simplicity: Easy to use for common cases
  2. Consistency: Similar patterns across the API
  3. Safety: Type-safe and error-resistant
  4. Performance: Efficient by default
  5. Extensibility: Easy to extend and customize

Doxygen Documentation

For the most up-to-date and detailed API documentation, generate the Doxygen docs using doxygen Doxyfile and browse the HTML output.

docs.title: Contributing to TwoDEngine
docs.text: Thank you for your interest in contributing to TwoDEngine! This guide will help you get started.

docs.title: Ways to Contribute
docs.text:
There are many ways to contribute to TwoDEngine:

  • 🐛 Report Bugs: Help us identify and fix issues
  • 💡 Suggest Features: Share ideas for new features
  • 📝 Improve Documentation: Fix typos, add examples, clarify explanations
  • 💻 Submit Code: Fix bugs or implement new features
  • 🎨 Create Examples: Share sample projects and tutorials
  • 🧪 Write Tests: Improve test coverage
  • 🌍 Translate: Help translate documentation

docs.title: 1. Fork the Repository
docs.text: Click the "Fork" button on the TwoDEngine GitHub page.

docs.title: 2. Clone Your Fork
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/TwoDEngine.git
cd TwoDEngine

docs.title: 3. Set Up Development Environment
docs.text:
# Run setup script
setup.bat  # Windows
./setup.sh # Linux/macOS

# Build the project
make

# Run tests
make test

docs.title: 4. Create a Branch
docs.text:
git checkout -b feature/your-feature-name
# or
git checkout -b fix/your-bug-fix

docs.title: Code Style
docs.text: TwoDEngine follows these coding standards:

docs.title: Naming Conventions
docs.text:
// Classes: PascalCase
class GameEngine { };

// Functions/Methods: PascalCase
void Initialize();
void ProcessInput();

// Variables: camelCase
int playerHealth;
float deltaTime;

// Constants: UPPER_SNAKE_CASE
const int MAX_ENTITIES = 1000;

// Private members: camelCase with no prefix
class Game
{
private:
    bool isRunning;  // Not m_isRunning or _isRunning
};

docs.title: Formatting
docs.text:
// Braces on new line
void Function()
{
    if (condition)
    {
        // Code here
    }
}

// Spaces around operators
int result = a + b;

// No spaces inside parentheses
Function(arg1, arg2);

// Indent with 4 spaces (no tabs)
void Function()
{
    if (condition)
    {
        DoSomething();
    }
}

docs.title: Comments
docs.text:
// Use Doxygen-style comments for public APIs
/**
 * @brief Brief description
 *
 * Detailed description here.
 *
 * @param paramName Parameter description
 * @return Return value description
 */
void Function(int paramName);

// Use // for inline comments
int value = 42;  // The answer to everything

// Use /* */ for multi-line explanations
/*
 * This is a complex algorithm that requires
 * multiple lines of explanation.
 */

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
All public APIs must be documented:

/**
 * @file Game.h
 * @brief Core game engine class
 */

/**
 * @class Game
 * @brief Main game engine class
 *
 * The Game class manages the entire game lifecycle including
 * initialization, the main game loop, and cleanup.
 *
 * @code
 * Game game;
 * game.Initialize();
 * game.Run();
 * game.Destroy();
 * @endcode
 */
class Game
{
public:
    /**
     * @brief Initialize the game engine
     *
     * Initializes all subsystems including SDL, window, renderer,
     * and asset manager.
     *
     * @throws std::runtime_error if initialization fails
     *
     * @see Run(), Destroy()
     */
    void Initialize();
};

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
Add tests for new features:

// tests/GameTest.cpp
#include "Game.h"
#include <gtest/gtest.h>

TEST(GameTest, InitializeSucceeds)
{
    Game game;
    EXPECT_NO_THROW(game.Initialize());
}

TEST(GameTest, DestroyIsIdempotent)
{
    Game game;
    game.Initialize();
    game.Destroy();
    EXPECT_NO_THROW(game.Destroy());  // Should not crash
}

docs.title: Commit Message Format
docs.text:
<type>(<scope>): <subject>

<body>

<footer>

docs.title: Types
docs.text:
• feat: New feature
  • fix: Bug fix
  • docs: Documentation changes
  • style: Code style changes (formatting, no logic change)
  • refactor: Code refactoring
  • test: Adding or updating tests
  • chore: Maintenance tasks

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
# Good commit messages
git commit -m "feat(renderer): add sprite batching support"
git commit -m "fix(input): handle controller disconnect properly"
git commit -m "docs(api): add examples to Game class documentation"

# Bad commit messages
git commit -m "fixed stuff"
git commit -m "WIP"
git commit -m "asdf"

docs.title: Detailed Commit Message
docs.text:
feat(audio): add spatial audio support

Implement 3D positional audio using SDL_mixer. This allows
sounds to be positioned in 3D space with distance attenuation
and stereo panning.

Features:
- Set sound position in 3D space
- Automatic distance attenuation
- Stereo panning based on position
- Configurable falloff curves

Closes #123

git fetch upstream
git rebase upstream/main

docs.title: 2. Run Tests

make clean && make

## Related Issues

docs.title: What to Expect
docs.text:
1. Initial Review: Within 2-3 days
  2. Feedback: Constructive comments and suggestions
  3. Iteration: Make requested changes
  4. Approval: At least one maintainer approval required
  5. Merge: Maintainer will merge your PR

docs.title: Basic Game Example
docs.text: This example demonstrates how to create a simple game with TwoDEngine, including window creation, input handling, and basic rendering.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: We'll create a simple game where a player-controlled square moves around the screen using keyboard input.

docs.title: Main.cpp
docs.text:
#include "Game.h"
#include <iostream>

int main(int argc, char* argv[])
{
    std::cout << "Starting Simple Game..." << std::endl;

    try {
        Game game;
        game.Initialize();
        game.Run();
        game.Destroy();
    }
    catch (const std::exception& e) {
        std::cerr << "Error: " << e.what() << std::endl;
        return 1;
    }

    std::cout << "Game exited successfully." << std::endl;
    return 0;
}

docs.title: Game.h
docs.text:
#ifndef GAME_H
#define GAME_H

#include <SDL2/SDL.h>

class Game
{
public:
    Game();
    ~Game();

    void Initialize();
    void Run();
    void ProcessInput();
    void Update();
    void Render();
    void Destroy();

private:
    SDL_Window* window;
    SDL_Renderer* renderer;
    bool isRunning;

    // Player properties
    SDL_Rect player;
    int playerSpeed;

    // Input state
    bool moveUp;
    bool moveDown;
    bool moveLeft;
    bool moveRight;
};

#endif // GAME_H

docs.title: Game.cpp
docs.text:
#include "Game.h"
#include <iostream>

Game::Game()
    : window(nullptr)
    , renderer(nullptr)
    , isRunning(false)
    , playerSpeed(200)
    , moveUp(false)
    , moveDown(false)
    , moveLeft(false)
    , moveRight(false)
{
    // Initialize player position and size
    player.x = 400;
    player.y = 300;
    player.w = 50;
    player.h = 50;
}

Game::~Game()
{
    Destroy();
}

void Game::Initialize()
{
    std::cout << "Initializing SDL..." << std::endl;

    // Initialize SDL
    if (SDL_Init(SDL_INIT_VIDEO) < 0)
    {
        throw std::runtime_error("SDL initialization failed: " + std::string(SDL_GetError()));
    }

    // Create window
    window = SDL_CreateWindow(
        "TwoDEngine - Basic Game",
        SDL_WINDOWPOS_CENTERED,
        SDL_WINDOWPOS_CENTERED,
        800,
        600,
        SDL_WINDOW_SHOWN
    );

    if (!window)
    {
        throw std::runtime_error("Window creation failed: " + std::string(SDL_GetError()));
    }

    // Create renderer
    renderer = SDL_CreateRenderer(window, -1, SDL_RENDERER_ACCELERATED | SDL_RENDERER_PRESENTVSYNC);

    if (!renderer)
    {
        throw std::runtime_error("Renderer creation failed: " + std::string(SDL_GetError()));
    }

    isRunning = true;
    std::cout << "Initialization complete!" << std::endl;
}

void Game::Run()
{
    std::cout << "Starting game loop..." << std::endl;

    Uint32 lastFrameTime = SDL_GetTicks();

    while (isRunning)
    {
        // Calculate delta time
        Uint32 currentTime = SDL_GetTicks();
        float deltaTime = (currentTime - lastFrameTime) / 1000.0f;
        lastFrameTime = currentTime;

        // Cap delta time to prevent large jumps
        if (deltaTime > 0.05f)
            deltaTime = 0.05f;

        ProcessInput();
        Update(deltaTime);
        Render();
    }

    std::cout << "Game loop ended." << std::endl;
}

void Game::ProcessInput()
{
    SDL_Event event;

    while (SDL_PollEvent(&event))
    {
        switch (event.type)
        {
            case SDL_QUIT:
                isRunning = false;
                break;

            case SDL_KEYDOWN:
                switch (event.key.keysym.sym)
                {
                    case SDLK_ESCAPE:
                        isRunning = false;
                        break;
                    case SDLK_w:
                    case SDLK_UP:
                        moveUp = true;
                        break;
                    case SDLK_s:
                    case SDLK_DOWN:
                        moveDown = true;
                        break;
                    case SDLK_a:
                    case SDLK_LEFT:
                        moveLeft = true;
                        break;
                    case SDLK_d:
                    case SDLK_RIGHT:
                        moveRight = true;
                        break;
                }
                break;

            case SDL_KEYUP:
                switch (event.key.keysym.sym)
                {
                    case SDLK_w:
                    case SDLK_UP:
                        moveUp = false;
                        break;
                    case SDLK_s:
                    case SDLK_DOWN:
                        moveDown = false;
                        break;
                    case SDLK_a:
                    case SDLK_LEFT:
                        moveLeft = false;
                        break;
                    case SDLK_d:
                    case SDLK_RIGHT:
                        moveRight = false;
                        break;
                }
                break;
        }
    }
}

void Game::Update(float deltaTime)
{
    // Update player position based on input
    if (moveUp)
        player.y -= static_cast<int>(playerSpeed * deltaTime);
    if (moveDown)
        player.y += static_cast<int>(playerSpeed * deltaTime);
    if (moveLeft)
        player.x -= static_cast<int>(playerSpeed * deltaTime);
    if (moveRight)
        player.x += static_cast<int>(playerSpeed * deltaTime);

    // Keep player within screen bounds
    if (player.x < 0) player.x = 0;
    if (player.y < 0) player.y = 0;
    if (player.x + player.w > 800) player.x = 800 - player.w;
    if (player.y + player.h > 600) player.y = 600 - player.h;
}

void Game::Render()
{
    // Clear screen (dark blue background)
    SDL_SetRenderDrawColor(renderer, 20, 30, 50, 255);
    SDL_RenderClear(renderer);

    // Draw player (white square)
    SDL_SetRenderDrawColor(renderer, 255, 255, 255, 255);
    SDL_RenderFillRect(renderer, &player);

    // Present frame
    SDL_RenderPresent(renderer);
}

void Game::Destroy()
{
    std::cout << "Cleaning up resources..." << std::endl;

    if (renderer)
    {
        SDL_DestroyRenderer(renderer);
        renderer = nullptr;
    }

    if (window)
    {
        SDL_DestroyWindow(window);
        window = nullptr;
    }

    SDL_Quit();

    std::cout << "Cleanup complete." << std::endl;
}

docs.title: Compile the Game
docs.text:
# Build using Make
make

# Or use the batch file (Windows)
build_and_run.bat

docs.title: Run the Game
docs.text:
# Windows
bin\main.exe

# Linux/macOS
./bin/main

docs.title: Controls
docs.text:
• Arrow Keys or WASD: Move the player
  • ESC: Exit the game
  • Close Window: Exit the game

docs.title: 1. Initialization
docs.text:
void Game::Initialize()
{
    // Initialize SDL video subsystem
    SDL_Init(SDL_INIT_VIDEO);

    // Create 800x600 window
    window = SDL_CreateWindow(...);

    // Create hardware-accelerated renderer with VSync
    renderer = SDL_CreateRenderer(...);
}

docs.title: 2. Game Loop
docs.text:
void Game::Run()
{
    while (isRunning)
    {
        // Calculate time since last frame
        float deltaTime = CalculateDeltaTime();

        // Process input, update, render
        ProcessInput();
        Update(deltaTime);
        Render();
    }
}

docs.title: 3. Input Handling
docs.text:
void Game::ProcessInput()
{
    SDL_Event event;
    while (SDL_PollEvent(&event))
    {
        // Handle keyboard events
        if (event.type == SDL_KEYDOWN)
        {
            // Set movement flags
            if (event.key.keysym.sym == SDLK_w)
                moveUp = true;
        }
    }
}

docs.title: 4. Update Logic
docs.text:
void Game::Update(float deltaTime)
{
    // Move player based on input
    if (moveUp)
        player.y -= playerSpeed * deltaTime;

    // Keep player in bounds
    if (player.x < 0) player.x = 0;
}

docs.title: 5. Rendering
docs.text:
void Game::Render()
{
    // Clear screen
    SDL_RenderClear(renderer);

    // Draw player
    SDL_RenderFillRect(renderer, &player);

    // Present frame
    SDL_RenderPresent(renderer);
}

docs.title: Enhancements
docs.text: Try adding these features to improve the game:

docs.title: Add a Background Color
docs.text:
void Game::Render()
{
    // Set background color (RGB)
    SDL_SetRenderDrawColor(renderer, 50, 100, 150, 255);
    SDL_RenderClear(renderer);

    // ... rest of rendering
}

docs.title: Add Player Color
docs.text:
void Game::Render()
{
    // Draw player in green
    SDL_SetRenderDrawColor(renderer, 0, 255, 0, 255);
    SDL_RenderFillRect(renderer, &player);
}

docs.title: Add Diagonal Movement
docs.text:
void Game::Update(float deltaTime)
{
    float moveX = 0.0f;
    float moveY = 0.0f;

    if (moveUp) moveY -= 1.0f;
    if (moveDown) moveY += 1.0f;
    if (moveLeft) moveX -= 1.0f;
    if (moveRight) moveX += 1.0f;

    // Normalize diagonal movement
    if (moveX != 0.0f && moveY != 0.0f)
    {
        float length = sqrt(moveX * moveX + moveY * moveY);
        moveX /= length;
        moveY /= length;
    }

    player.x += static_cast<int>(moveX * playerSpeed * deltaTime);
    player.y += static_cast<int>(moveY * playerSpeed * deltaTime);
}

docs.title: Add Frame Rate Display
docs.text:
void Game::Render()
{
    // ... existing rendering code

    // Calculate FPS
    static int frameCount = 0;
    static Uint32 lastTime = SDL_GetTicks();
    frameCount++;

    Uint32 currentTime = SDL_GetTicks();
    if (currentTime - lastTime >= 1000)
    {
        std::cout << "FPS: " << frameCount << std::endl;
        frameCount = 0;
        lastTime = currentTime;
    }
}

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Now that you have a basic game running, try these tutorials:

  • Loading Images: Display sprites instead of rectangles
  • Adding Sound: Add sound effects and music
  • Multiple Objects: Create multiple game objects
  • Collision Detection: Detect when objects collide

Congratulations!

You've created your first game with TwoDEngine! This example demonstrates the core concepts you'll use in all your games.

docs.title: Building TwoDEngine
docs.text: This guide covers different ways to build TwoDEngine and explains the build system in detail.

docs.title: Build System Overview
docs.text: TwoDEngine uses GNU Make as its primary build system. The Makefile is configured to automatically discover and compile all source files in the src/ directory.

docs.title: Windows
docs.text:
• MinGW-w64: GCC compiler for Windows
  • Make: GNU Make (included with MinGW)
  • Git: For version control

docs.title: Linux
docs.text: sudo apt-get install build-essential git

docs.title: macOS
docs.text:
xcode-select --install
brew install make

docs.title: Method 1: Using Make (Recommended)
docs.text:
The Makefile provides several targets for building and managing the project:

# Build the project
make

# Clean build artifacts
make clean

# Rebuild from scratch
make clean && make

# Build with verbose output
make VERBOSE=1

docs.title: Method 2: Using Batch Files (Windows Only)
docs.text: TwoDEngine provides convenient batch files for common workflows:

docs.title: build_and_run.bat
docs.text:
Builds the project and runs the executable: 

build_and_run.bat

docs.title: build_run_engine.bat
docs.text:
Builds, runs, and packages the engine: 

build_run_engine.bat

docs.title: pack_engine.bat
docs.text:
Creates a distributable package: 

pack_engine.bat

docs.title: Compiler Flags
docs.text:
The Makefile uses the following compiler flags:

# C++ Standard
-std=c++17

# Optimization
-O2                    # Optimize for speed

# Warnings
-Wall                  # Enable all warnings
-Wextra               # Enable extra warnings

# Include paths
-Ilibs                # Library headers
-Ilibs/imgui          # ImGui headers
-Ilibs/glm            # GLM headers
-Ilibs/sol            # Sol2 headers
-Ilibs/lua            # Lua headers
-Ipybind11-2.11.1/include  # pybind11 headers

docs.title: Linker Flags
docs.text:
# SDL2 Libraries
-lmingw32 -lSDL2main -lSDL2 -lSDL2_image -lSDL2_ttf -lSDL2_mixer

# Lua Library
-llua54

# Python Library
-lpython310

# System Libraries
-lopengl32            # OpenGL
-lgdi32               # Windows GDI

docs.title: Source File Discovery
docs.text:
The Makefile automatically finds all .cpp files in the src/ directory:

# Automatically find all .cpp files in src directory
SRC_FILES = $(wildcard src/*.cpp)

# Combine with library sources
SRC = $(SRC_FILES) \
      libs/imgui/imgui.cpp \
      libs/imgui/imgui_demo.cpp \
      libs/imgui/imgui_draw.cpp \
      libs/imgui/imgui_widgets.cpp \
      libs/imgui/imgui_sdl.cpp

This means you can add new .cpp files to the src/ directory without modifying the Makefile!

docs.title: Output Directory
docs.text:
All build artifacts are placed in the bin/ directory:

TARGET = bin/main.exe

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
The Makefile tracks header dependencies automatically:

# Generate dependency files
-MMD -MP

docs.title: Default Target (all)
docs.text:
make
# or simply
make
Builds the main executable.

docs.title: Clean Target
docs.text:
make clean
Removes all build artifacts: - Object files (*.o) - Dependency files (*.d) - Executable (bin/main.exe)

docs.title: Rebuild Target
docs.text:
make rebuild
Equivalent to make clean && make.

docs.title: Debug Build
docs.text:
To build with debug symbols and no optimization:

make CFLAGS="-g -O0 -Wall -Wextra"

docs.title: Release Build
docs.text:
For maximum optimization:

make CFLAGS="-O3 -DNDEBUG -Wall -Wextra"

docs.title: Parallel Build
docs.text:
Speed up compilation using multiple cores:

make -j4  # Use 4 cores
make -j   # Use all available cores

docs.title: Build Output
docs.text:
A successful build produces:

bin/
├── main.exe              # Main executable
├── SDL2.dll              # SDL2 library
├── SDL2_image.dll        # SDL2_image library
├── SDL2_ttf.dll          # SDL2_ttf library
├── SDL2_mixer.dll        # SDL2_mixer library
└── lua54.dll             # Lua library

docs.title: Issue: "command not found: make"
docs.text:
Solution: Install Make 

# Windows (using Chocolatey)
choco install make

# Linux
sudo apt-get install make

# macOS
brew install make

docs.title: Issue: "undefined reference to SDL_*"
docs.text:
Solution: Ensure SDL2 libraries are in the correct location 

# Run setup script
setup.bat  # Windows
./setup.sh # Linux/macOS

docs.title: Issue: "cannot find -lSDL2"
docs.text:
Solution: Check library paths in Makefile 

LDFLAGS = -Llibs/SDL2/lib -Llibs/lua -Lpython-3.10.11

docs.title: Issue: Compilation is slow
docs.text:
Solution: Use parallel compilation 

make -j4

docs.title: Issue: Changes not reflected in build
docs.text:
Solution: Clean and rebuild 

make clean && make

docs.title: Build Performance Tips
docs.text:
1. Use parallel builds: make -j
  2. Use ccache: Cache compilation results
  3. Incremental builds: Only modified files are recompiled
  4. Precompiled headers: Consider using PCH for large projects

docs.title: GitHub Actions Example
docs.text:
name: Build

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: windows-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v2
      - name: Setup MinGW
        uses: egor-tensin/setup-mingw@v2
      - name: Run Setup
        run: setup.bat
      - name: Build
        run: make
      - name: Test
        run: bin/main.exe

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Running the Engine
  • Project Structure
  • Contributing

Build System

TwoDEngine uses GNU Make for its simplicity and cross-platform compatibility. Future versions may include CMake support for more advanced build configurations.

docs.title: Quick Start Guide
docs.text: This guide will help you get TwoDEngine up and running in just a few minutes.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before you begin, ensure you have the following installed:

  • Git: For cloning the repository
  • MinGW-w64 (Windows) or GCC (Linux/macOS): C++ compiler
  • Make: Build automation tool

docs.title: Step 1: Clone the Repository
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/yourusername/TwoDEngine.git
cd TwoDEngine

docs.title: Step 2: Run Setup Script
docs.text: TwoDEngine includes a convenient setup script that downloads and configures all dependencies.

docs.title: Windows
docs.text: setup.bat

docs.title: Linux/macOS
docs.text:
chmod +x setup.sh
./setup.sh

The setup script will:

  • Download SDL2 and related libraries
  • Download Lua 5.4
  • Download Python 3.10 (if needed)
  • Download pybind11
  • Configure all dependencies
  • Verify the installation

Setup Complete

If the setup completes successfully, you'll see a message indicating all dependencies are ready.

docs.title: Using Make
docs.text:
The simplest way to build TwoDEngine is using the provided Makefile:

# Build the engine
make

# Clean build artifacts
make clean

# Rebuild from scratch
make clean && make

docs.title: Using Batch Files (Windows)
docs.text:
TwoDEngine provides convenient batch files for common tasks:

# Build and run the engine
build_and_run.bat

# Build, run, and package the engine
build_run_engine.bat

docs.title: Your First Program
docs.text: Let's create a simple program that initializes the game engine.

docs.title: 1. Create Main.cpp
docs.text:
Create a file src/Main.cpp with the following content:

#include "Game.h"
#include <iostream>

int main(int argc, char* argv[])
{
    std::cout << "Starting TwoDEngine..." << std::endl;

    // Create game instance
    Game game;

    // Initialize the engine
    game.Initialize();

    // Run the game loop
    game.Run();

    // Clean up resources
    game.Destroy();

    std::cout << "TwoDEngine shutdown complete." << std::endl;

    return 0;
}

docs.title: 2. Create Game.h
docs.text:
Create a file src/Game.h:

#ifndef GAME_H
#define GAME_H

/**
 * @class Game
 * @brief Main game engine class
 */
class Game
{
public:
    Game();
    ~Game();

    void Initialize();
    void Run();
    void ProcessInput();
    void Update();
    void Render();
    void Destroy();

private:
    bool isRunning;
};

#endif // GAME_H

docs.title: 3. Create Game.cpp
docs.text:
Create a file src/Game.cpp:

#include "Game.h"
#include <iostream>

Game::Game() : isRunning(false)
{
    std::cout << "Game constructor called" << std::endl;
}

Game::~Game()
{
    std::cout << "Game destructor called" << std::endl;
}

void Game::Initialize()
{
    std::cout << "Initializing game..." << std::endl;
    isRunning = true;
}

void Game::Run()
{
    std::cout << "Starting game loop..." << std::endl;

    while (isRunning)
    {
        ProcessInput();
        Update();
        Render();

        // For now, exit after one iteration
        isRunning = false;
    }
}

void Game::ProcessInput()
{
    // TODO: Handle input
}

void Game::Update()
{
    // TODO: Update game logic
}

void Game::Render()
{
    // TODO: Render graphics
}

void Game::Destroy()
{
    std::cout << "Cleaning up resources..." << std::endl;
}

docs.title: 4. Build and Run
docs.text:
# Build the project
make

# Run the executable
./bin/main.exe  # Windows
./bin/main      # Linux/macOS

You should see output similar to:

Starting TwoDEngine...
Game constructor called
Initializing game...
Starting game loop...
Cleaning up resources...
Game destructor called
TwoDEngine shutdown complete.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Congratulations! You've successfully set up TwoDEngine and created your first program. Here's what to explore next:

  • Creating a Window

    Learn how to create an SDL window and renderer

    Window Creation

  • Handling Input

    Process keyboard, mouse, and controller input

    Input Handling

  • Loading Assets

    Load and display images, fonts, and sounds

    Asset Management

  • Using Lua Scripts

    Add scripting capabilities to your game

    Lua Scripting

docs.title: Build Errors
docs.text:
If you encounter build errors, try:

  1. Clean and rebuild: make clean && make
  2. Verify dependencies: Run setup.bat again
  3. Check compiler: Ensure MinGW or GCC is in your PATH

docs.title: Missing DLLs (Windows)
docs.text:
If you get "missing DLL" errors when running:

  1. Ensure all DLLs are in the bin/ directory
  2. Run setup.bat to copy DLLs automatically
  3. Check that SDL2, Lua, and Python DLLs are present

docs.title: Linker Errors
docs.text:
If you get undefined reference errors:

  1. Verify all .cpp files are listed in the Makefile
  2. Check that library paths are correct
  3. Ensure all required libraries are linked

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
If you're stuck, here are some resources:

  • Documentation: Browse the User Guide
  • API Reference: Check the API Documentation
  • Examples: Look at Example Projects
  • GitHub Issues: Search or create an issue on GitHub

Pro Tip

Use the provided batch files (build_and_run.bat, build_run_engine.bat) for faster development iterations on Windows!

docs.title: User Guide Overview
docs.text: Welcome to the TwoDEngine User Guide! This comprehensive guide will help you master all aspects of game development with TwoDEngine.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text: This guide covers everything from basic concepts to advanced techniques for creating 2D games with TwoDEngine.

docs.title: 🎯 Core Concepts
docs.text:
Learn the fundamental concepts and architecture of TwoDEngine:

  • Game Loop: Understanding the main game loop pattern
  • Entity-Component System: Modern game architecture
  • Asset Management: Loading and managing game resources
  • Scene Management: Organizing game content

docs.title: 🎮 Game Development
docs.text:
Master the essential features for game development:

  • Window Management: Creating and managing game windows
  • Input Handling: Keyboard, mouse, and controller input
  • Graphics Rendering: 2D sprite rendering and effects
  • Audio System: Sound effects and background music
  • Physics: Collision detection and response

docs.title: 🔧 Advanced Topics
docs.text:
Explore advanced features and optimization techniques:

  • Scripting: Lua and Python integration
  • Performance: Optimization and profiling
  • Debugging: Tools and techniques
  • Networking: Multiplayer support (planned)

docs.title: Quick Navigation
docs.text:
• Window & Rendering

    Create windows and render graphics

    Learn More

  • Input Handling

    Process user input from various devices

    Learn More

  • Asset Management

    Load and manage game assets

    Learn More

  • Lua Scripting

    Add scripting to your games

    Learn More

  • Python Integration

    Extend your game with Python

    Learn More

  • Audio System

    Add sound effects and music

    Learn More

docs.title: Beginner Path
docs.text:
1. Quick Start: Set up your first project
  2. Window Management: Create a game window
  3. Input Handling: Handle keyboard and mouse
  4. Asset Management: Load images and fonts
  5. Basic Rendering: Draw sprites to screen

docs.title: Intermediate Path
docs.text:
1. Animation System: Animate sprites
  2. Audio System: Add sound effects
  3. Collision Detection: Implement physics
  4. Lua Scripting: Add game logic scripts
  5. UI System: Create game menus

docs.title: Advanced Path
docs.text:
1. Entity-Component System: Advanced architecture
  2. Performance Optimization: Optimize your game
  3. Python Integration: Extend with Python
  4. Custom Shaders: Advanced graphics
  5. Debugging Tools: Profile and debug

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
Throughout this guide, you'll find practical code examples:

// Example: Creating a simple game window
#include "Game.h"

int main(int argc, char* argv[])
{
    Game game;
    game.Initialize();
    game.Run();
    game.Destroy();
    return 0;
}

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
Each section includes:

  • ✅ Recommended patterns: What to do
  • ❌ Anti-patterns: What to avoid
  • 💡 Tips: Helpful hints and tricks
  • ⚠️ Warnings: Common pitfalls

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
If you get stuck:

  1. Search this guide: Use the search bar at the top
  2. Check API Reference: See API Documentation
  3. View Examples: Browse Example Projects
  4. Ask the Community: Visit GitHub Discussions

docs.title: Contributing to the Guide
docs.text:
Found an error or want to improve the documentation?

  • Report Issues: Open an issue on GitHub
  • Submit Improvements: Create a pull request
  • Share Examples: Contribute code examples

See the Contributing Guide for details.

Interactive Learning

The best way to learn is by doing! Try the code examples as you read through the guide.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: Ready to dive in? Start with the Quick Start Guide to create your first TwoDEngine project!
